concepts.title: Terms related to parabola
concepts.conceptBody:
Some terms related to parabola

(a) Axis : The axis of a parabola is the straight line through the focus and perpendicular to the directrix. In the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$, $\text{x}$-axis is the axis.

If the point $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ lies on the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$, the point $\left( \text{x},-\text{y} \right)$ also lies on it.

Hence the parabola is symmetrical about its axis.

(b) Vertex : The point of intersection of the parabola and its axis is called its vertex. In the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax},\text{O}\left( 0,0 \right)$ i.e., the origin is the vertex.

(c) Double ordinate : If PN be drawn perpendicular to the axis and produced to meet the curve again at $\text{{P}'}$, then $\text{P P}$ is called a double ordinate.

(d) Latus Rectum : The double ordinate LSL' which passes through the focus is called the latus rectum of the parabola. The equation of the latus rectum of parabola ${{y}^{2}}=4ax$, is $x=a$. Solving $\text{x}=\text{a}$ and ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$, we find the co-ordinates of the extremities of the latus rectum which are $\text{L}\left( \text{a},2\text{a} \right)$ and $\text{{L}'}\left( \text{a},-2\text{a} \right)$. Since $LS={L}'S=2a$, therefore length of the latus rectum ${L}'{L}'=4a$.

(e) Chord : A cord of a parabola is the line segment joining any two points on the parabola.

(f) Focal chord : a chord of a parabola passing through the focus is called a focal chord. In the given figure $\text{Q}{{\text{Q}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$ and $\text{L}{{\text{L}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$ are focal chords.

(g) Focal distance : The focal distance of any point $\text{Q}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ on the parabola is its distance from the focus i.e., QS.

Also QS = QM = distance of $\text{Q}$ from the directrix. $\{$ as e $=1\}$

For the parabola ${{y}^{2}}=4ax,a>0$.

(h) focus is (a, 0) (ii) Vertex is $\left( 0,0 \right)$ (iii) directrix is $\text{x}=-\text{a}$ (iv) axis is $\text{x}$-axis

i.e., $\text{y}=0$ (v) length of latus rectum $=4\text{a}$ (vi) equation of latus rectum is $\text{x}=\text{a}$

(i) extremities of latus rectum are $\left( a,2a \right)$ and $\left( a,-2a \right)$ (viii) focal distance of

$Q\left( x,y \right)$ is $\left( x+a \right)$.

concepts.title: Tranformation to standard form
concepts.conceptBody:
Reduction of equation of a parabola into standard form 

  • The equation ${{(y-\beta )}^{2}}=4\text{a}\left( \text{x}-\alpha \right)$ can be reduced to the standard form by the transformations $\text{x}-\alpha =\text{X},\text{y}-\beta =\text{Y}$.

The equation becomes ${{\text{Y}}^{2}}=4\text{aX}$, which is the standard form in $\text{X},\text{Y}$ coordinates.

${{(y-\beta )}^{2}}=4a\left( x-\alpha \right)$ is the form of equation of a parabola whose axis of

symmetry is parallel to the x-axis.

  • The equation ${{(ax+by+c)}^{2}}=bx-ay+{c}'$ can be reduced to the standard form by the transformations

$\frac{ax+by+c}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}=Y,\frac{bx-ay+{c}'}{\sqrt{{{b}^{2}}+{{a}^{2}}}}=X$

[ Note : The lines ax $+by+c=0$ and $bx-ay+{c}'=0$ are perpendicular to each other].

The equation becomes ${{\text{Y}}^{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}}\text{X}$ which is the standard form in $\text{X},\text{Y}$ coordinates.
